description: You are acting as a junior backup administrator who wants to automate a very small-scale “archive & clean” workflow with make. Your goal is to set up one self-contained project directory called /home/user/backup_demo that fulfils the following specification: 1. Directory structure & sample data • Inside /home/user/backup_demo create a) a sub-directory named data that holds exactly three text files ‑ report1.txt (the literal single line: Report 1: Q1 results) ‑ report2.txt (the literal single line: Report 2: Q2 results) ‑ log.txt  (the literal single line: System logs for archival) b) a sub-directory named backups (initially empty). 2. Makefile requirements • Place a standard GNU-make compatible file named exactly /home/user/backup_demo/Makefile. • It must define exactly two user-visible, phony targets called backup and clean. (Declare them with “.PHONY: backup clean” so that make will not try to look for files of those names.) • Target behaviour: backup : ‑ ensures the directory backups exists (create it if missing). ‑ creates/overwrites a compressed archive called /home/user/backup_demo/backups/data_backup.tar.gz that contains the *contents* of data (i.e. inside the archive the three files must appear at the archive root, not under a “data/” prefix). ‑ appends the literal string “data_backup.tar.gz” (followed by a newline) to a log file called /home/user/backup_demo/backup.log. clean : ‑ removes every “*.tar.gz” file inside backups. ‑ does **not** touch any other file. • Implementation may be done in as little as 2-3 shell commands per target; do **not** copy the sample files themselves—only archive them. 3. One demonstration run After authoring the Makefile, execute make backup once from inside /home/user/backup_demo. Upon completion, the following files must exist so the automated checker can verify them: a) /home/user/backup_demo/backups/data_backup.tar.gz b) /home/user/backup_demo/backup.log which must contain exactly one line: data_backup.tar.gz 4. Output formats the test harness will check • Archive content list (when extracted) must be **exactly** report1.txt, report2.txt, log.txt • Each file’s line endings should be ordinary Unix “\n”. • The log file must contain the single byte sequence “data_backup.tar.gz\n”. Do *not* move, rename or add extra files beyond what is described, otherwise the automated grader will fail. All work must be done with normal user privileges under /home/user.
truth:
Filesystem objects that must be present after successful task completion:

1. /home/user/backup_demo/data/report1.txt  
   Contents: (15 bytes + newline)  
   “Report 1: Q1 results\n”

2. /home/user/backup_demo/data/report2.txt  
   Contents: (15 bytes + newline)  
   “Report 2: Q2 results\n”

3. /home/user/backup_demo/data/log.txt  
   Contents: (26 bytes + newline)  
   “System logs for archival\n”

4. /home/user/backup_demo/Makefile  
   Must contain:
     - a line that begins exactly “.PHONY: backup clean”
     - a rule whose header begins exactly “backup:” (case-sensitive)
     - within that rule a command that invokes “tar” with “czf” and produces “backups/data_backup.tar.gz”
     - a command that appends the literal string “data_backup.tar.gz” to “backup.log”
     - a rule “clean:” that deletes “backups/*.tar.gz”

5. /home/user/backup_demo/backup.log  
   Contents (exact, 18 bytes incl. newline):  
   “data_backup.tar.gz\n”

6. /home/user/backup_demo/backups/data_backup.tar.gz  
   The archive, when extracted, produces exactly the three files named in items 1-3 with their correct contents; no directory prefix inside the archive.

Directory permissions may be default 755; file permissions 644. No root access is required.
